Group 9  ·  Compiler Architecture Assignment

Custom RISC-V Instruction
Attention Mechanism (attn)

Modifying the RISC-V GNU Toolchain to add a hardware instruction for Transformer Attention

▸ PART 1  —  WHAT, WHY & ENVIRONMENT SETUP
The Big Picture

What exactly did we build?

Objective

We added a brand-new CPU instruction called attn to the RISC-V architecture. This instruction computes the full Transformer Attention formula:

Attention(Q, K, V)  =  Softmax( QKᵀ / √d_k ) · V

A C program can call this as a single built-in function — __builtin_riscv_attn(q, k, v) — and the compiler will emit a single attn instruction in the binary.

Why build a custom instruction?

On a normal CPU, computing attention requires dozens of instructions: multiply loops, a division, an exponential, normalization, another multiply loop. A custom instruction collapses all of that into one opcode. On real custom silicon (like an FPGA or an AI chip), that single instruction would fire dedicated hardware circuits — running in one clock cycle instead of hundreds.

For this assignment, we verify the instruction at the compiler level: we prove the compiler knows about it, can emit it, and it shows up correctly in the disassembly.

How — the three layers we touched
riscv-opcodesdefines the 32-bit binary encoding of the instruction
Binutils (assembler + disassembler)teaches gas to assemble "attn" and objdump to disassemble it
GCC (compiler)adds __builtin_riscv_attn so C code can emit the instruction without inline assembly
Expected Questions
Why not just use inline assembly?
The assignment explicitly forbids it. Also, a built-in is cleaner — it lets GCC optimize register allocation around the instruction.
Does the instruction actually execute the math?
At the compiler level (our scope), no. The instruction is emitted and verified via objdump. Actual execution would require a hardware simulator (QEMU) or real chip.
What is RISC-V? Why are we modifying its toolchain?
RISC-V is an open-source CPU instruction set. Its toolchain (GCC + Binutils) is open source, making it the standard platform for adding experimental custom instructions.
Step 1 — Install Dependencies

Install all required system libraries

Objective

Prepare the Linux/WSL environment with every library needed to compile GCC and Binutils from source.

Command
sudo apt-get update sudo apt-get install -y autoconf automake autotools-dev curl python3 \ libmpc-dev libmpfr-dev libgmp-dev gawk build-essential \ bison flex texinfo gperf libtool patchutils bc \ zlib1g-dev libexpat-dev device-tree-compiler
Why each group of packages?
autoconf, automake, libtoolGCC's build system is GNU Autotools — these are required to run ./configure
libmpc, libmpfr, libgmpGCC needs these math libraries internally to handle arbitrary-precision arithmetic during compilation
bison, flexParser generators — GCC's frontend uses them to parse C source code
build-essential, python3Core C/C++ compiler (to compile GCC itself) and Python (used by the riscv-opcodes parser)
NOTE  This step is identical whether you are on Ubuntu natively or using WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux). Run it once before anything else.
Why do we compile GCC from source instead of just downloading it?
We are modifying GCC's internal source files. A pre-built binary won't have our changes. We must compile our patched version ourselves.
How long does the build take?
15–40 minutes depending on CPU cores. We use -j$(nproc) to parallelize across all available cores.
Step 2 — Create Workspace & Clone Toolchain

Set up folder structure and download the source code

Objective

Create a clean working directory and download the RISC-V GNU Toolchain source code along with only the submodules we need (Binutils and GCC).

Commands
# Create our working directory mkdir -p ~/dc && cd ~/dc # Clone the toolchain (--depth 1 = only latest snapshot, saves ~3 GB) git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/riscv-collab/riscv-gnu-toolchain cd riscv-gnu-toolchain # Initialize only binutils and qemu submodules (not all — saves time) git submodule update --init --depth 1 binutils git submodule update --init --depth 1 qemu
Why

--depth 1 (shallow clone) downloads only the latest commit, not the entire git history. The full history is several gigabytes — we don't need it since we're patching the current version.

We initialize only the binutils and gcc submodules because those are the only ones we modify. The full toolchain has many more submodules (newlib, gdb, etc.) that would take extra time.

Clone the RISC-V Opcodes tool

Objective

Download the official RISC-V opcode definition repository. This is a separate tool used to formally define instruction encodings and auto-generate the MATCH and MASK hex constants.

Command
cd ~/dc git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/riscv/riscv-opcodes
Why a separate opcodes repo?

RISC-V instructions are 32-bit words. The layout of bits — which bits are the opcode, which are registers — follows a strict format. The riscv-opcodes tool lets us describe our instruction in human-readable form and automatically computes the correct hex encoding constants. Without it, we'd have to calculate bit fields by hand — error-prone and tedious.

What is ~/dc?
"dc" stands for our working directory name. It can be anything — we just use it to keep all our files in one place.
Why do we need riscv-opcodes separately? Isn't it inside the toolchain?
The toolchain has its own opcode tables, but riscv-opcodes is the canonical definition tool. We use it to generate correct MATCH/MASK values, then copy those into the toolchain manually.
What is a submodule?
A git submodule is a separate git repository embedded inside another. The toolchain repo links to specific versions of binutils, gcc, gdb etc. as submodules.

<meta charset="UTF-8"/>

<title>Custom RISC-V Instruction — Part 2</title>
<style>
  @import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;600&family=Syne:wght@400;600;700;800&display=swap');

  :root {
    --bg:        #0d0f14;
    --surface:   #13161e;
    --border:    #1e2330;
    --blue:      #4f8ef7;
    --blue-dim:  #1a2a4a;
    --teal:      #2ec4a9;
    --teal-dim:  #0d2e28;
    --amber:     #f0a500;
    --amber-dim: #2e2100;
    --red:       #e05555;
    --red-dim:   #2e1010;
    --green:     #4caf7d;
    --green-dim: #0d2a1a;
    --purple:    #9b7df0;
    --purple-dim:#1e1540;
    --text:      #e4e7f0;
    --muted:     #6b7280;
    --mono:      'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
    --sans:      'Syne', sans-serif;
  }

  * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }

  body {
    background: var(--bg);
    color: var(--text);
    font-family: var(--sans);
    font-size: 15px;
    line-height: 1.7;
    padding: 48px 24px;
  }

  .page {
    max-width: 860px;
    margin: 0 auto;
  }

  /* ── Header ── */
  .header {
    border-left: 4px solid var(--blue);
    padding-left: 20px;
    margin-bottom: 52px;
  }
  .header .tag {
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 11px;
    color: var(--blue);
    text-transform: uppercase;
    letter-spacing: 2px;
    margin-bottom: 8px;
  }
  .header h1 {
    font-size: 32px;
    font-weight: 800;
    color: #fff;
    line-height: 1.2;
  }
  .header h1 span { color: var(--blue); }
  .header p {
    color: var(--muted);
    margin-top: 8px;
    font-size: 14px;
  }

  /* ── Part label ── */
  .part-label {
    display: inline-flex;
    align-items: center;
    gap: 10px;
    background: var(--teal-dim);
    border: 1px solid var(--teal);
    color: var(--teal);
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 12px;
    font-weight: 600;
    padding: 6px 14px;
    border-radius: 4px;
    margin-bottom: 36px;
    letter-spacing: 1px;
  }

  /* ── Section heading ── */
  .section {
    margin-bottom: 48px;
  }
  .section-title {
    font-size: 11px;
    font-family: var(--mono);
    text-transform: uppercase;
    letter-spacing: 2.5px;
    color: var(--muted);
    margin-bottom: 20px;
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    gap: 10px;
  }
  .section-title::after {
    content: '';
    flex: 1;
    height: 1px;
    background: var(--border);
  }

  /* ── Step card ── */
  .step-card {
    background: var(--surface);
    border: 1px solid var(--border);
    border-radius: 8px;
    padding: 24px 28px;
    margin-bottom: 16px;
    position: relative;
  }
  .step-card::before {
    content: attr(data-step);
    position: absolute;
    top: -1px;
    left: -1px;
    background: var(--teal);
    color: #000;
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 10px;
    font-weight: 600;
    padding: 3px 10px;
    border-radius: 7px 0 6px 0;
    letter-spacing: 1px;
  }

  .step-card h3 {
    font-size: 16px;
    font-weight: 700;
    color: #fff;
    margin-bottom: 6px;
    margin-top: 10px;
  }

  /* ── Pill labels ── */
  .pill {
    display: inline-block;
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 10px;
    font-weight: 600;
    padding: 2px 9px;
    border-radius: 99px;
    margin-bottom: 14px;
    letter-spacing: 0.5px;
  }
  .pill-obj  { background: var(--blue-dim);   color: var(--blue);   border: 1px solid var(--blue); }
  .pill-how  { background: var(--teal-dim);   color: var(--teal);   border: 1px solid var(--teal); }
  .pill-why  { background: var(--purple-dim); color: var(--purple); border: 1px solid var(--purple); }
  .pill-cmd  { background: var(--amber-dim);  color: var(--amber);  border: 1px solid var(--amber); }

  /* ── Subsection inside step ── */
  .sub {
    margin-top: 16px;
    padding-top: 16px;
    border-top: 1px solid var(--border);
  }
  .sub-label {
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 10px;
    font-weight: 600;
    letter-spacing: 1.5px;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    margin-bottom: 8px;
  }
  .sub-label.obj    { color: var(--blue); }
  .sub-label.how    { color: var(--teal); }
  .sub-label.why    { color: var(--purple); }

  p { margin-bottom: 8px; }
  p:last-child { margin-bottom: 0; }

  /* ── Code block ── */
  .code-block {
    background: #080a0e;
    border: 1px solid var(--border);
    border-left: 3px solid var(--amber);
    border-radius: 6px;
    padding: 16px 18px;
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 12.5px;
    line-height: 1.8;
    overflow-x: auto;
    margin-top: 10px;
  }
  .code-block .comment { color: var(--muted); }
  .code-block .cmd     { color: var(--amber); }
  .code-block .arg     { color: var(--teal); }
  .code-block .flag    { color: var(--purple); }
  .code-block .path    { color: var(--blue); }
  .code-block .kw      { color: var(--purple); }
  .code-block .val     { color: var(--green); }
  .code-block .str     { color: var(--teal); }
  .code-block .num     { color: var(--amber); }

  /* ── Info / Note box ── */
  .note {
    background: var(--blue-dim);
    border: 1px solid var(--blue);
    border-radius: 6px;
    padding: 14px 18px;
    font-size: 13.5px;
    margin-top: 14px;
    color: #b8d0ff;
  }
  .note strong { color: var(--blue); font-family: var(--mono); }

  .warn {
    background: var(--amber-dim);
    border: 1px solid var(--amber);
    border-radius: 6px;
    padding: 14px 18px;
    font-size: 13.5px;
    margin-top: 14px;
    color: #ffe0a0;
  }
  .warn strong { color: var(--amber); font-family: var(--mono); }

  .error-box {
    background: var(--red-dim);
    border: 1px solid var(--red);
    border-radius: 6px;
    padding: 14px 18px;
    font-size: 13.5px;
    margin-top: 14px;
    color: #ffb0b0;
  }
  .error-box strong { color: var(--red); font-family: var(--mono); }
  .error-box .fix { color: var(--green); margin-top: 6px; }
  .error-box .fix strong { color: var(--green); font-family: var(--mono); }

  /* ── QnA ── */
  .qna {
    background: var(--surface);
    border: 1px solid var(--border);
    border-radius: 8px;
    overflow: hidden;
    margin-top: 8px;
  }
  .qna-item {
    padding: 14px 20px;
    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);
  }
  .qna-item:last-child { border-bottom: none; }
  .qna-q {
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 12px;
    color: var(--amber);
    margin-bottom: 5px;
    font-weight: 600;
  }
  .qna-q::before { content: 'Q  '; opacity: 0.5; }
  .qna-a {
    font-size: 13.5px;
    color: #c5cfe0;
    line-height: 1.6;
  }
  .qna-a::before {
    content: 'A  ';
    font-family: var(--mono);
    font-size: 12px;
    color: var(--teal);
    font-weight: 600;
  }

  /* ── Flow diagram ── */
  .flow {
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
    gap: 0;
    margin-top: 16px;
  }
  .flow-item {
    display: flex;
    align-items: stretch;
    gap: 0;
  }
  .flow-dot {
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
    align-items: center;
    width: 40px;
    flex-shrink: 0;
  }
  .flow-dot .dot {
    width: 12px;
    height: 12px;
    border-radius: 50%;
    background: var(--blue);
    border: 2px solid var(--bg);

=== part2_opcode_and_binutils-1776242957043-sqdav4.html ===
    Group 9  ·  Compiler Architecture Assignment 
  Custom RISC-V Instruction 
 Attention Mechanism (attn) 
  Modifying the RISC-V GNU Toolchain to add a hardware instruction for Transformer Attention 
  
  ▸ PART 2  —  OPCODE DEFINITION & BINUTILS PATCHING 
      Where We Are 
  
[CONTEXT]  The three-layer pipeline — picking up from Part 1 
    
 
 
  ✔ Done — riscv-opcodes We define the 32-bit binary encoding of the attn instruction here first 
  
   
 
 
  ▸ Now — Binutils (assembler + disassembler) We teach gas to assemble "attn" and objdump to recognise it 
  
   
 
 
  Up next — GCC (compiler) We add __builtin_riscv_attn so C code can emit the instruction 
  
  
  Part 2 covers Steps 1 – 4 of the implementation: defining the instruction's bit encoding in riscv-opcodes , propagating those constants into Binutils ( riscv-opc.h and riscv-opc.c ), and doing a standalone Binutils build to verify the assembler before touching GCC. 
  
  
       Step 1 — Define the Instruction Encoding (riscv-opcodes) 
  
[STEP 1.1]  Add the attn entry to the custom extension file 
   Objective 
  Formally declare the attn instruction in the RISC-V opcode definition language so the official parser can auto-generate the correct MATCH and MASK hex constants we will later copy into Binutils. 
  
   How — file to edit and the exact line to add 
  File:  ~/dc/riscv-opcodes/extensions/unratified/rv_custom 
   # Append this single line to the end of rv_custom:  attn  rd rs1 rs2 rs3  26..25=0x0  14..12=0x0  6..2=0x02  1..0=3  
  
   Why — decoding every field 
  Each token maps directly to a group of bits in the 32-bit RISC-V instruction word. Here is what every field means: 
    31-30 
 funct2 
 26..25=0x0 
 
  29-27 
 rs3 
 
  14-12 
 funct3 
 = 0x0 
 
  24-20 
 rs2 
 
  19-15 
 rs1 
 
  11-7 
 rd 
 
  6-2 
 opcode 
 custom-0 
 
  1-0 
 11 
 = 3 
 
  
    
 
 
  rd rs1 rs2 rs3 Four register operands → this is an R4-type instruction (like FMA in floating-point). attn needs Q, K, V inputs and produces one output. 
  
   
 
 
  6..2=0x02 (custom-0 opcode slot) RISC-V reserves four opcode slots (custom-0 through custom-3) specifically for vendor extensions. We use custom-0 to guarantee zero conflict with any standard instruction. 
  
   
 
 
  26..25=0x0 and 14..12=0x0 The funct2 and funct3 sub-opcode fields. Setting them to zero selects the first (and only) instruction in this custom slot, leaving room to define up to 32 more custom instructions later using different funct values. 
  
   
 
 
  1..0=3 All standard 32-bit RISC-V instructions end in binary 11. This is the "quadrant" marker that distinguishes 32-bit encodings from 16-bit compressed instructions. 
  
  
  
  
  
[STEP 1.2]  Run the opcode parser to generate the constants 
   Objective 
  Use the Python-based riscv-opcodes build system to parse our new definition and generate the MATCH_ATTN and MASK_ATTN hex constants. 
  
   Commands 
   cd  ~/dc/riscv-opcodes  make  # This runs the Python parser across all extension files  # and writes encoding constants to encoding.out.h  # Verify attn appears in the output:  grep  ATTN  encoding.out.h  
  You should see output like: 
   #define  MATCH_ATTN  0xb  #define  MASK_ATTN  0x600707f  
  
   Why these specific hex values? 
  MATCH_ATTN = 0xb is the 32-bit pattern of the instruction with all register fields zeroed out — it's the "fingerprint" of the opcode itself. 
  MASK_ATTN = 0x600707f is a bitmask that tells the assembler which bits are fixed (the opcode and funct fields) versus which bits vary (the register numbers). The assembler ANDs any instruction word with this mask and checks if the result equals MATCH_ATTN to identify the instruction. 
  
   ERROR ENCOUNTERED: -bash: ./parse-opcodes: No such file or directory FIX: Older tutorials reference a now-removed shell script. The current riscv-opcodes repo uses a Python + Makefile pipeline instead. Simply run make from inside the repo — it calls the Python scripts automatically. 
  
  
    Why use the custom-0 slot specifically? 
  RISC-V explicitly reserves opcodes custom-0 through custom-3 for non-standard extensions. Using them guarantees our instruction will never clash with any ratified or future standard instruction. 
  
   Why does RISC-V need a 4-register (R4-type) instruction? 
  Standard R-type instructions only support 2 source registers. Attention needs 3 inputs (Q, K, V). The R4-type format (used by floating-point fused multiply-add instructions) was the natural fit — it uses the same rs3 field encoding. 
  
   What does MASK actually do? 
  It isolates only the fixed bits (opcode, funct2, funct3). When the assembler or disassembler reads a binary instruction, it does: (instruction_word AND MASK) == MATCH? → if yes, it's "attn". The register fields are variable, so they must be masked out before comparing. 
  
   What is "unratified"? Why is our file there? 
  The riscv-opcodes repo organises extensions into ratified (officially approved by RISC-V International) and unratified (experimental/vendor-defined). Our custom instruction belongs in the unratified folder since it's a project-specific extension. 
  
  
  
       Step 2 — Patch Binutils Opcode Header (riscv-opc.h) 
  
[STEP 2]  Add MATCH / MASK constants and DECLARE_INSN to the Binutils header 
   Objective 
  Copy the generated constants into the Binutils header file so that the assembler ( gas ) and disassembler ( objdump ) can recognise the attn instruction when they encounter it in assembly source or binary object files. 
  
   How — file to edit 
  File:  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain/binutils/include/opcode/riscv-opc.h 
  Find the block where other MATCH / MASK constants are defined (search for any existing MATCH_F instruction) and add your three lines nearby in alphabetical order: 
   /* Custom attn instruction — added by Group 9 */  #define  MATCH_ATTN  0xb  #define  MASK_ATTN  0x600707f  DECLARE_INSN ( attn , MATCH_ATTN , MASK_ATTN ) 
  
   Why three separate declarations? 
    
 
 
  #define MATCH_ATTN The 32-bit bit-pattern of the instruction with all register fields = 0. Used for pattern recognition at decode time. 
  
   
 
 
  #define MASK_ATTN The bitmask that isolates the fixed bits. Used together with MATCH to identify the instruction: (word & MASK) == MATCH. 
  
   
 
 
  DECLARE_INSN(attn, ...) A Binutils macro that registers the instruction into Binutils' internal opcode table data structure — linking the name "attn" to its MATCH and MASK values so they can be referenced elsewhere in the codebase. 
  
  
  
   NOTE The values here are copied directly from the encoding.out.h generated in Step 1. Do not compute them manually. The Python parser guarantees correctness. 
  
    What is riscv-opc.h? Who reads it? 
  It's the master header that declares all RISC-V instruction constants for Binutils. Both the assembler (gas) and the disassembler (objdump) include this header at compile time, so any instruction listed here becomes known to both tools simultaneously. 
  
   What is DECLARE_INSN exactly? Is it a function? 
  No — it's a C preprocessor macro. Depending on where it's included in the build, it expands to different things: sometimes a struct initialiser, sometimes an extern declaration. This single macro call handles all the different usage sites automatically. 
  
   Can we just hard-code random hex values instead of using the parser? 
  Technically yes, but it's risky. If you miscalculate even one bit, the assembler will silently emit wrong binary. The riscv-opcodes parser derives MATCH and MASK from the human-readable field definition mathematically, guaranteeing correctness. 
  
  
  
       Step 3 — Patch Binutils Opcode Source (riscv-opc.c) 
  
[STEP 3]  Register attn in the assembler's instruction table 
   Objective 
  Add a full instruction descriptor entry to the assembler's opcode table. This is what allows gas to actually assemble the mnemonic "attn" into machine code and objdump to print it back as "attn" when disasse

=== part3_gcc_backend-1776242954586-jjpd85.html ===
    Custom RISC-V Instruction · Group 9 · Attention Mechanism 
  Part 3 — GCC Backend Patching 
  Teaching the C compiler to emit the custom attn instruction from pure C code — no inline assembly required. 
  
   ◈ PART 3 of 4  ·  GCC BACKEND — FTYPES · BUILTINS · MACHINE DESCRIPTION 
    riscv-opcodes bit encoding 
  Binutils assembler / objdump 
  GCC Backend C → custom opcode 
  Build & Verify final proof 
  
    What This Part Covers 
  
[OVERVIEW]  How GCC turns a C function call into machine code 
   Objective 
  By the end of Part 2, the assembler knows about attn . But GCC — the C compiler — still has no idea it exists. If you write __builtin_riscv_attn(q, k, v) in a C file right now, GCC will refuse to compile it. 
  Part 3 patches exactly three GCC source files to close that gap: we define a function type signature, register a built-in, and write a machine description pattern. After this, GCC will emit the custom opcode directly from C. 
  
   How — the three-file chain inside GCC 
    FILE 1   riscv-ftypes.def  Defines the C-level type signature: 3 floats in → 1 float out  
  
  ▼ 
   FILE 2   riscv-builtins.cc  Registers __builtin_riscv_attn and links it to the internal pattern  
  
  ▼ 
   FILE 3   riscv.md  Maps the internal RTL pattern → the "attn" assembly string  
  
  ▼ 
   OUTPUT   GCC emits the custom opcode  from plain C — no inline assembly  
  
  
  
   Why three separate files? 
  GCC's backend is modular by design. The type system , built-in registry , and instruction patterns are kept separate so they can be maintained independently. Adding a new instruction requires touching all three layers — skip any one and GCC will fail to compile, link, or emit the instruction correctly. 
  
  
  
       Step 5 — GCC Function Type Definition 
  
[STEP 05]  Register the type signature: float fn(float, float, float) 
   Objective 
  Tell GCC's type system that our built-in accepts three single-precision float arguments and returns one single-precision float . GCC uses a registry of pre-declared type signatures — if our signature isn't there, the compiler can't create the function prototype for __builtin_riscv_attn .

  RISCV_SF_FTYPE_SF_SF_SF This is the auto-generated enum name for the type signature we declared in Step 5. GCC's macro system turns DEF_RISCV_FTYPE(3,(SF,SF,SF,SF)) into this enum constant automatically — we just reference it here. 
  
   
 
 
  hard_float A GCC availability predicate — this built-in is only enabled when the target supports hardware floating-point (the F extension). On a target without FP hardware, calling __builtin_riscv_attn will produce a helpful compile-time error. 
  
  
  
   ERROR ENCOUNTERED: 'RISCV_ATYPE_SF' was not declared in this scope ROOT CAUSE: The RISC-V built-in registration system had ATYPE macros only for integer types. Floating-point types like SF were not previously needed in that context. 
 
 FIX APPLIED: Added #define RISCV_ATYPE_SF float_type_node alongside the existing ATYPE definitions. This maps the SF type code to GCC's built-in tree node for the C float type. 
  
  
    What exactly is float_type_node? 
  It's a GCC-internal C variable that holds a pointer to the pre-built tree node representing the C "float" type. GCC represents all types (int, float, pointers, etc.) as nodes in an internal tree structure. By setting RISCV_ATYPE_SF = float_type_node, we're telling GCC: "when you see SF in a type signature, that means C float." 
  
   What does DIRECT_BUILTIN do differently from other built-in types? 
  DIRECT_BUILTIN tells GCC to map the function call 1-to-1 to a single machine instruction pattern (defined in riscv.md). There's no function prologue, no argument passing through memory — the compiler just substitutes the call with the raw instruction at the point of use. This is the most efficient kind of built-in. 
  
   How does RISCV_SF_FTYPE_SF_SF_SF get its name? 
  GCC auto-generates enum names from DEF_RISCV_FTYPE entries using a naming convention: RISCV_{return_type}_FTYPE_{arg1}_{arg2}_{arg3}. So DEF_RISCV_FTYPE(3,(SF,SF,SF,SF)) produces the enum constant RISCV_SF_FTYPE_SF_SF_SF — return SF, 3 args all SF. 
  
  
  
       Step 7 — 

=== part4_build_and_verification-1776242951947-cqopni.html ===
      Custom RISC-V Instruction · Group 9 · Attention Mechanism 
  Part 4 — Full Build & Verification 
  Compile the modified toolchain, write a C test, and confirm the custom opcode appears in disassembly 
  
  ▸ PART 4 of 4  ·  Final Stage 
    Where we are in the pipeline 
   PART 1 Setup & Opcodes 
  → 
  PART 2 Binutils Patches 
  → 
  PART 3 GCC Backend 
  → 
  PART 4 Build & Verify ✔ 
  
   NOTE All source edits are complete. Parts 1–3 defined the instruction in three layers (encoding, assembler, compiler). Part 4 does two things: compile everything into real binaries , then prove the instruction was generated correctly using objdump. 
  
       Step 9 — Full Toolchain Build & Install 
  
[STEP 09]  Compile the entire patched GCC + Binutils and install to ~/riscv_custom 
   Objective 
  Translate all the source code edits we made across Parts 1–3 into actual executable binaries: a cross-compiler ( riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc ) and a disassembler ( riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump ). Until this step, our changes exist only as text files — this step bakes them into working tools. 
  
   Commands 
   # Navigate to the top-level toolchain directory  cd  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain  # Configure: set target architecture and install prefix  ./configure  --prefix= ~/riscv_custom  --with-arch= rv64imfd  --with-abi= lp64d  # Build using all available CPU cores (fastest)  make  -j$(nproc)  # Install the built binaries to ~/riscv_custom/bin/  make install  
  
   Breaking down each flag 
    
 
 
  --prefix=~/riscv_custom Installs everything into our own folder — no root privileges needed, easy to delete/redo 
  
   
 
 
  --with-arch=rv64imfd Targets 64-bit RISC-V with Integer, Multiply, single-precision Float (F), and double-precision Float (D) extensions — required because our instruction is floating-point 
  
   
 
 
  --with-abi=lp64d Uses the LP64D calling convention where floats are passed in floating-point registers — necessary for our 3-float-argument instruction to use the correct registers (fa0, fa1, fa2) 
  
   
 
 
  -j$(nproc) Parallel compilation using all CPU cores. nproc returns the number of cores available. Cuts build time from ~40 min down to ~15 min on a 4-core machine 
  
  
  
   Why a full rebuild is mandatory 
  GCC and Binutils are themselves written in C/C++. The files we edited — riscv-builtins.cc , riscv.md , riscv-opc.c — are source files. They must be compiled into object files and linked into the final GCC and Binutils executables. There is no shortcut: a partial build might leave stale .o files that don't include our new instruction. A clean full build guarantees consistency. 
  
   TIP — Build Failing? If make -j$(nproc) fails with a cryptic error, re-run with make -j1 . Parallel builds can hide the real error message under a flood of output. Single-threaded output makes the exact failing line visible. 
  
    What does "make install" actually copy, and where? 
  It copies the built executables — riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc, riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump, riscv64-unknown-elf-as, and others — into ~/riscv_custom/bin/. It also copies header files and libraries needed by those tools. 
  
   Why rv64imfd and not just rv64i? 
  Our instruction is a floating-point instruction in the F extension class (INSN_CLASS_F). Without enabling the F and D extensions at configure time, GCC won't emit floating-point register names (fa0, fa1, fa2) and will refuse to compile code that uses float arguments. 
  
   How long does the build take? 
  Approximately 15–40 minutes depending on hardware. A 4-core machine at -j4 typically completes in about 20 minutes. The build compiles hundreds of C++ source files including GCC's entire backend. 
  
  
  
       Step 10 — Write the Verification C Program 
  
[STEP 10]  Write main.c — invoke __builtin_riscv_attn from pure C 
   Objective 
  Write a small C program that calls the custom built-in function. This will prove the entire chain works: from a standard C function call, the compiler should emit the attn machine instruction — with no inline assembly. 
  
   The C code — main.c 
   // main.c — Custom RISC-V attn instruction verification  float  run_attention ( float q, float k, float v) { // Call the built-in — no inline assembly used  return __builtin_riscv_attn(q, k, v);
} int  main () { // volatile forces GCC to actually emit the instruction  // (prevents Dead Code Elimination at -O2)  volatile float result = run_attention(1.0f, 2.0f, 3.0f); return 0;
} 
  
   Why volatile? — Dead Code Elimination 
  At -O2 optimization, GCC performs Dead Code Elimination (DCE) . If the compiler determines that the result of a computation is never used, it removes the computation entirely — including our custom instruction call. volatile tells the compiler: "this value has side-effects, never remove this assignment." This forces the attn instruction to actually appear in the binary. 
  We also isolate the call in a run_attention wrapper function. This prevents inlining from hiding the instruction within a larger optimization pass. 
  
   GOTCHA If you write float r = __builtin_riscv_attn(q,k,v); directly in main() and never print r , GCC at -O2 will silently delete the whole call. Always use volatile when verifying with optimizations. 
  
    Why are we compiling with -O2 at all? Couldn't we just use -O0? 
  -O0 (no optimization) would work for basic verification. We use -O2 deliberately to show that our instruction survives standard optimization levels — which is closer to real-world use. The volatile trick is the correct industrial approach to force-emit an instruction under optimization. 
  
   What does __builtin_riscv_attn actually compute at runtime? 
  At the compiler level, it just emits the opcode. On real hardware or a simulator, the hardware would compute the attention operation. Since we're verifying at the toolchain level (no simulator), the instruction is emitted but not executed — which is exactly within our scope. 
  
  
  
       Step 10 (cont.) — Compile & Disassemble 
  
[STEP 10b]  Use our custom GCC to compile main.c, then verify with objdump 
   Objective 
  Invoke the freshly built cross-compiler on main.c , then use the cross-disassembler to read back the object file and confirm that attn fa0, fa0, fa1, fa2 appears in the disassembly output. 
  
   Commands 
   # Step A — compile main.c to an object file  ~/riscv_custom/bin/riscv64-unknown-elf-gcc  -O2 -c  main.c  -o  main.o  # Step B — disassemble the object file  ~/riscv_custom/bin/riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump  -d  main.o  
  
   Flag breakdown 
    Flag Meaning    -O2 Optimization level 2 — standard production-level optimizations. Tests that our instruction survives real optimization passes.  -c Compile only, do not link. Produces a .o (object) file. We don't need a full executable — we just need the machine code.  -d Disassemble — translate raw machine bytes back into assembly mnemonics using the Binutils opcode tables we patched in Part 2.    
   Expected objdump output 
  The key section to look for in the disassembly output is the run_attention function: 
   Disassembly of section .text:  0000000000000000 <run_attention>:  0:  60b5050b  attn  fa0,fa0,fa1,fa2  ← ✔ custom instruction emitted  4:  8082  ret  0000000000000002 <main>:  ...  34:  68e7878b  attn  fa5,fa5,fa4,fa3  38:  00f12627  fsw  fa5,12(sp)  
  The mnemonic attn appears in the disassembly. This is definitive proof that: 
    
 
 
  GCC emitted the correct 32-bit opcode The hex value encodes opcode=custom-0, plus funct3 and funct2 fields we defined in Part 1 
  
   
 
 
  Binutils decoded it back correctly objdump used the MATCH/MASK constants from riscv-opc.h (Part 2) to recognise the bit pattern and print "attn" 
  
   
 
 
  No inline assembly was used The source is pure C calling __builtin_riscv_attn — the assignment constraint is fully satisfied

  That means Binutils doesn't recognise the opcode

Step 7 — GCC Machine Description (riscv.md) 
  
[STEP 07]  Define the RTL → assembly mapping: internal pattern to "attn %0,%1,%2,%3" 
   Objective 
  The Machine Description file ( riscv.md ) is where GCC's internal representation (called RTL — Register Transfer Language ) is finally translated into actual assembly strings. This step creates the pattern that GCC uses to emit the attn mnemonic when it encounters our built-in in a C program. 
  
   How — two edits in riscv.md 
  File:  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain/gcc/gcc/config/riscv/riscv.md 
  Edit A — Add UNSPEC_ATTN to the unspec enum 
  Find the existing define_c_enum "unspec" block at the top of riscv.md. Add UNSPEC_ATTN inside it alongside the other UNSPEC_* entries: 
   ; Inside the define_c_enum "unspec" [ ... ] block:  UNSPEC_ATTN  ; custom attention instruction — Group 9  
  Edit B — Add the define_insn pattern (at the end of the file, before the closing comment): 
   ;; Custom attn instruction — Attention Mechanism (Group 9) ( define_insn  "riscv_attn" [( set ( match_operand : SF  0  "register_operand"  "=f" )
       ( unspec : SF [( match_operand : SF  1  "register_operand"  "f" )
                   ( match_operand : SF  2  "register_operand"  "f" )
                   ( match_operand : SF  3  "register_operand"  "f" )] UNSPEC_ATTN ))] "TARGET_HARD_FLOAT"  "attn\t%0,%1,%2,%3" [( set_attr  "type"  "fmadd" )]) 
  
   Why — decoding the define_insn pattern 
    
 
 
  define_insn "riscv_attn" Names this pattern "riscv_attn" — this exact string is what riscv-builtins.cc looks up when it processes our DIRECT_BUILTIN(attn, ...) entry. The names must match. 
  
   
 
 
  match_operand:SF 0 "register_operand" "=f" Operand 0 is the destination — a single-float register (SF), constrained to float register class ("f"). The "=" means it's written (output). Operands 1, 2, 3 are inputs ("f" without "=" = read only). 
  
   
 
 
  unspec:SF [...] UNSPEC_ATTN UNSPEC (unspecified operation) is GCC's way of representing custom, opaque operations that the optimizer should treat as a black box. UNSPEC_ATTN is our unique identifier — it prevents GCC from accidentally merging or eliminating our instruction during optimization. 
  
   
 
 
  "attn\t%0,%1,%2,%3" — the output template This is the actual string GCC writes to the assembly file. %0 through %3 are substituted with the actual allocated register names at code-generation time (e.g., fa0, fa1, fa2, fa3). 
  
   
 
 
  set_attr "type" "fmadd" Assigns this instruction to the "fmadd" pipeline scheduling category — the same category as the fused multiply-add instruction, since our instruction also involves a multi-operand floating-point computation. This matters for the instruction scheduler. 
  
  
  
   ERROR ENCOUNTERED: unknown value 'fmac' for attribute 'type' ROOT CAUSE: An older tutorial used 'fmac' as the pipeline type. This category was renamed or removed in the GCC version we cloned. 
 
 FIX APPLIED: Changed the type attribute to "fmadd" — which is the valid pipeline category in the current GCC version for multi-operand floating-point instructions. 
  
  
    What is RTL (Register Transfer Language)? 
  RTL is GCC's internal intermediate representation — a low-level description of operations in terms of register reads, writes, and memory accesses. GCC first converts your C code to RTL, optimizes it, then converts RTL to assembly using the machine description patterns in riscv.md. 
  
   Why do we use UNSPEC instead of describing the actual arithmetic? 
  UNSPEC is used because the attention computation is opaque to GCC — the optimizer doesn't know or need to know what the instruction does mathematically. If we described it as regular arithmetic (multiply, add, etc.), GCC might try to "optimize" it, split it, or substitute it with other instructions. UNSPEC tells GCC: "emit this exactly as-is, treat it as a black box." 
  
   Why does the pattern name "riscv_attn" need to match DIRECT_BUILTIN(attn, ...)? 
  GCC's built-in lookup uses a naming convention: DIRECT_BUILTIN(attn, ...) automatically looks for a define_insn named "riscv_attn" (it prepends "riscv_"). If the name doesn't match, GCC cannot find the pattern and will fail to generate code for the built-in call. 
  
   What does TARGET_HARD_FLOAT do? 
  It's a guard condition — the pattern is only activated when the compilation target has hardware floating-point support. If someone tries to compile with -msoft-float, this pattern is silently disabled and GCC gives an error about the built-in being unavailable, which is exactly the correct behavior.

[RECAP]  How a C call becomes the attn opcode — end to end 
   The complete journey 
   /* C source code (what the programmer writes) */  float  run_attention ( float q, float k, float v) { return  __builtin_riscv_attn (q, k, v); // Step 6: registered in builtins.cc } 
  ↓   GCC frontend parses C — looks up __builtin_riscv_attn in built-in table 
 ↓   Finds type signature RISCV_SF_FTYPE_SF_SF_SF  (Step 5: ftypes.def) 
 ↓   Maps to pattern "riscv_attn" via DIRECT_BUILTIN  (Step 6: builtins.cc) 
 ↓   Instantiates the define_insn with allocated registers  (Step 7: riscv.md) 
 ↓   Writes assembly string to .s file 
   ; Generated assembly (what GCC emits)  attn  fa0 , fa0 , fa1 , fa2  
  
   Summary table — three files, three roles 
     File  Role  What we added      riscv-ftypes.def  Type registry  DEF_RISCV_FTYPE(3,(SF,SF,SF,SF)) — declares the float→float×3 signature    riscv-builtins.cc  C API bridge  RISCV_ATYPE_SF + DIRECT_BUILTIN(attn,...) — exposes __builtin_riscv_attn    riscv.md  Instruction emitter  define_insn "riscv_attn" — emits "attn %0,%1,%2,%3" from RTL     
   ✔ After these three edits — GCC knows about __builtin_riscv_attn , understands its type, and will emit the correct 32-bit custom opcode whenever it is called from C. The next step is to rebuild the full toolchain and verify using objdump.

What if the output shows ".insn" or a hex number instead of "attn"? 
  That means Binutils doesn't recognise the opcode — the MATCH_ATTN / MASK_ATTN constants in riscv-opc.h or the entry in riscv-opc.c were not applied before the build, or the build was not fully re-run. The fix is to verify the patches in Part 2 are present and re-run make. 
  
   Why does the instruction appear twice — in run_attention and in main? 
  At -O2, GCC inlines the run_attention call into main as well as keeping the standalone function. Both occurrences are valid — it shows the instruction emits correctly in both inlined and non-inlined contexts. The volatile on the main-side call is what keeps the main copy alive. 
  
   Does the hex opcode 60b5050b mean anything specific? 
  Yes. In binary: bits 6–0 = 0x0b (custom-0 opcode), bits 14–12 = 000 (funct3=0), bits 26–25 = 00 (funct2=0). The remaining bits encode the destination (fa0) and source registers (fa0, fa1, fa2). This exactly matches the encoding we defined in rv_custom in Part 1. 
  
  
  
       Errors Encountered & How We Fixed Them 
  These are the real errors hit during implementation — each is included because they represent common pitfalls anyone following this process will likely encounter. 
   
[ERROR: ERROR 01]  -bash: ./parse-opcodes: No such file or directory 
   Root Cause 
  Online tutorials reference a parse-opcodes bash script that existed in older versions of the riscv-opcodes repository. Recent versions removed it in favour of a Python-based make infrastructure. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Used the new Python-based approach: run make inside the riscv-opcodes directory instead of calling the old script. This invokes parse_opcodes.py automatically and generates the correct header output. 
   # Instead of: ./parse-opcodes rv_custom rv32_i ... > encoding.out  # Use the Makefile target directly:  cd  ~/dc/riscv-opcodes  make  
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 02]  grep for MATCH_ATTN returns empty — constants missing from header 
   Root Cause 
  The custom instruction entry in the rv_custom extension file had a missing trailing newline or extra whitespace. The Python parser silently skipped the malformed line, so the generated header contained no MATCH_ATTN or MASK_ATTN definitions. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Manually opened the file in a text editor and verified the entry was on its own clean line with no leading/trailing spaces and a proper newline at the end of the file, then re-ran make in riscv-opcodes. 
   # Verify the line looks exactly like this (no trailing spaces):  attn rd rs1 rs2 rs3 26..25=0x0 14..12=0x0 6..2=0x02 1..0=3  # Check the generated output contains MATCH_ATTN:  grep  MATCH_ATTN  encoding.out  
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 03]  make: *** No rule to make target 'all-binutils' 
   Root Cause 
  The top-level toolchain wrapper Makefile wraps binutils targets under a versioned internal name. In certain toolchain versions, calling make all-binutils from the top level fails because the target name has changed. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Performed a standalone binutils build by entering the binutils submodule directory directly, running its own configure script, and building from there. 
   cd  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain/binutils  mkdir  build  && cd  build  ../configure  --target= riscv64-unknown-elf  --prefix= ~/riscv_custom  make  -j$(nproc)  make install  
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 04]  unknown value 'fmac' for attribute 'type' 
   Root Cause 
  The riscv.md  define_insn block initially used (set_attr "type" "fmac") to declare the instruction's pipeline scheduling category. The GCC version in the toolchain does not define an fmac attribute in its scheduler — only fmadd exists. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Changed the type attribute from "fmac" to "fmadd" in the define_insn block in riscv.md . 
   ; In gcc/config/riscv/riscv.md — change this:  [(set_attr "type" "fmac")]  ; To this:  [(set_attr "type" "fmadd")]  
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 05]  'RISCV_ATYPE_SF' was not declared in this scope 
   Root Cause 
  The RISC-V built-in registration infrastructure uses RISCV_ATYPE_* macros to map type codes from ftypes.def to GCC internal type nodes. The SF (Single Float) code is only used for integer types in the vanilla toolchain — it had never been extended to float types, so no mapping existed for RISCV_ATYPE_SF . 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Added a #define mapping in riscv-builtins.cc to connect the SF type code to GCC's float_type_node (which represents the C float type). 
   // In gcc/config/riscv/riscv-builtins.cc  // Add alongside the other ATYPE definitions:  #define  RISCV_ATYPE_SF float_type_node 
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 06]  attn instruction missing from objdump output 
   Root Cause 
  The initial test code called __builtin_riscv_attn directly in main() without using the result and without volatile . At -O2 , GCC's Dead Code Elimination pass determined that the call had no observable side effects and silently removed it from the output. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Two changes: (1) wrapped the intrinsic in a dedicated run_attention() function, and (2) declared the result as volatile float in main to mark it as having observable side effects — preventing DCE from removing it.

What if the output shows ".insn" or a hex number instead of "attn"? 
  That means Binutils doesn't recognise the opcode — the MATCH_ATTN / MASK_ATTN constants in riscv-opc.h or the entry in riscv-opc.c were not applied before the build, or the build was not fully re-run. The fix is to verify the patches in Part 2 are present and re-run make. 
  
   Why does the instruction appear twice — in run_attention and in main? 
  At -O2, GCC inlines the run_attention call into main as well as keeping the standalone function. Both occurrences are valid — it shows the instruction emits correctly in both inlined and non-inlined contexts. The volatile on the main-side call is what keeps the main copy alive. 
  
   Does the hex opcode 60b5050b mean anything specific? 
  Yes. In binary: bits 6–0 = 0x0b (custom-0 opcode), bits 14–12 = 000 (funct3=0), bits 26–25 = 00 (funct2=0). The remaining bits encode the destination (fa0) and source registers (fa0, fa1, fa2). This exactly matches the encoding we defined in rv_custom in Part 1.

[ERROR: ERROR 01]  -bash: ./parse-opcodes: No such file or directory

  Online tutorials reference a parse-opcodes bash script that existed in older versions of the riscv-opcodes repository. Recent versions removed it in favour of a Python-based make infrastructure. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Used the new Python-based approach: run make inside the riscv-opcodes directory instead of calling the old script. This invokes parse_opcodes.py automatically and generates the correct header output. 
   # Instead of: ./parse-opcodes rv_custom rv32_i ... > encoding.out  # Use the Makefile target directly:  cd  ~/dc/riscv-opcodes  make  
  
  
   
[ERROR: ERROR 02]  grep for MATCH_ATTN returns empty — constants missing from header 
   Root Cause 
  The custom instruction entry in the rv_custom extension file had a missing trailing newline or extra whitespace. The Python parser silently skipped the malformed line, so the generated header contained no MATCH_ATTN or MASK_ATTN definitions. 
  
   Fix Applied 
  Manually opened the file in a text editor and verified the entry was on its own clean line with no leading/trailing spaces and a proper newline at the end of the file, then re-ran make in riscv-opcodes.

Step 3 — Patch Binutils Opcode Source (riscv-opc.c) 
  
[STEP 3]  Register attn in the assembler's instruction table 
   Objective 
  Add a full instruction descriptor entry to the assembler's opcode table. This is what allows gas to actually assemble the mnemonic "attn" into machine code and objdump to print it back as "attn" when disassembling. 
  
   How — file to edit and entry to add 
  File:  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain/binutils/opcodes/riscv-opc.c 
  Find the section containing other floating-point instructions (e.g., search for fadd.s or fmadd.s ) and add the following entry in a logical position: 
   /* Custom attn instruction — Group 9 */ { "attn" , 0 , INSN_CLASS_F , "D,S,T,R" , MATCH_ATTN , MASK_ATTN , match_opcode , 0 }, 
  
   Why — decoding each field of the entry 
    
 
 
  "attn" The mnemonic string. This is what the programmer writes in assembly, and what objdump prints during disassembly. 
  
   
 
 
  0 (ISA version) Minimum ISA version field — 0 means "no specific version requirement". Our instruction is new, so it has no version dependency. 
  
   
 
 
  INSN_CLASS_F The instruction class — "F" means single-precision floating-point. This tells the assembler to only allow this instruction when the F extension is active (which it is for our target). 
  
   
 
 
  "D,S,T,R" (operand format string) Each letter maps to a register type. D = destination float register (rd), S = source float register (rs1), T = source float register (rs2), R = source float register (rs3). This 4-operand string matches our R4-type encoding exactly. 
  
   
 
 
  match_opcode The matching function — a standard Binutils helper that performs the (word & MASK) == MATCH check. We don't need a custom function since our instruction has no special decode logic. 
  
  
  
   GOTCHA Ensure there is a trailing newline at the very end of the file after your addition. Missing newlines have caused the grep verification in Step 1.2 to silently fail in some parser versions — the entry is present but not found because it's attached to the last line without a newline terminator. 
  
    What is riscv-opc.c and how is it different from riscv-opc.h? 
  The .h header declares the constants (MATCH/MASK) and is included across the codebase. The .c source file contains the actual opcode table — an array of structs — that the assembler and disassembler search at runtime to look up instructions. Both must be updated. 
  
   What does INSN_CLASS_F mean exactly? 
  INSN_CLASS_F tells the assembler this instruction requires the RISC-V "F" (single-precision float) ISA extension. The assembler will reject it with an error if the target doesn't support floating-point. Our toolchain targets riscv64-unknown-elf with the F extension enabled. 
  
   Why "D,S,T,R" and not something like "rd,rs1,rs2,rs3"? 
  Binutils uses a compact single-character operand format code. D/S/T/R are the standard codes for floating-point registers in R4-type instructions (the same codes used by fmadd.s etc.). Integer registers would use "d,s,t" (lowercase). Using the standard codes lets Binutils handle register parsing and printing automatically. 
  
  
  
       Step 4 — Standalone Binutils Build & Verification 
  
[STEP 4]  Build only Binutils to verify the assembler before touching GCC 
   Objective 
  Compile and install only Binutils (not the full toolchain yet) to confirm that gas correctly assembles the attn mnemonic and objdump can print it back — before investing 30+ minutes in a full GCC rebuild. 
  
   Commands 
   # Navigate into the binutils submodule directory  cd  ~/dc/riscv-gnu-toolchain/binutils  # Create a build directory (always build out-of-tree)  mkdir  -p  build-binutils  && cd  build-binutils  # Configure for RISC-V bare-metal target  ../configure  --target= riscv64-unknown-elf  --prefix= ~/riscv_custom  # Build (use all CPU cores)  make  -j$(nproc)  # Install the built binaries to prefix  make install  
  
   Verify with a quick assembly test 
   # Write a minimal assembly file  echo  "attn fa0, fa0, fa1, fa2"  >  /tmp/test_attn.s  # Assemble it using our newly-built cross-assembler  ~/riscv_custom/bin/riscv64-unknown-elf-as  -march=rv64gc  /tmp/test_attn.s  -o  /tmp/test_attn.o  # Disassemble and check the output  ~/riscv_custom/bin/riscv64-unknown-elf-objdump  -d  /tmp/test_attn.o  
  Expected objdump output: 
   Disassembly of section .text:  0000000000000000 < .text >: 0 : 60b5050b  attn  fa0,fa0,fa1,fa2  
  
   Why build Binutils standalone first? 
  A full GCC + Binutils build takes 15–40 minutes. If there's a typo or syntax error in our Binutils patches, a standalone Binutils build catches it in under 2 minutes. This fail-fast strategy saves enormous rebuild time. 
  Additionally, a successful standalone Binutils build with correct objdump output proves the encoding is right before we proceed to the more complex GCC modifications in Part 3. 
  
   ERROR ENCOUNTERED: make: *** No rule to make target 'all-binutils' FIX: This error occurs when trying to build Binutils via the top-level riscv-gnu-toolchain Makefile. That wrapper hides submodule targets in certain versions. The fix: navigate directly into the binutils/ subdirectory and run ./configure + make there independently, as shown in the commands above. 
  
  
    What is "out-of-tree" building and why do we do it? 
  Out-of-tree means running ./configure and make from a separate build/ directory rather than inside the source tree. This keeps generated build artifacts out of the source files, making it easy to do a clean rebuild by just deleting the build directory — without touching your source changes. 
  
   What does --target=riscv64-unknown-elf mean? 
  It's the target triple: architecture (riscv64) + vendor (unknown) + OS/ABI (elf, meaning bare-metal, no OS). This produces a cross-assembler that runs on your x86 Linux machine but generates RISC-V binary code. 
  
   Why does objdump show "60b5050b" as the hex encoding? 
  That's the 32-bit machine code of "attn fa0,fa0,fa1,fa2". The lowest 7 bits (0x0b = 0000 1011 in binary) encode the custom-0 opcode. The register numbers (fa0=10, fa1=11, fa2=12) are packed into the rs1, rs2, rs3, rd fields. The MATCH value 0xb is the pattern with all registers zero — adding register numbers changes the other bits. 
  
   After this step, can we compile C code with attn yet? 
  Not yet. The assembler knows about "attn", but GCC (the C compiler) doesn't know how to emit it from a C function call. That requires patching GCC's backend — covered in Part 3. 
  
  
  
    Group 9 · Attention Mechanism · Custom RISC-V Instruction  PART 2 of 4  ▸  next: GCC Backend Patching

Base directory for this skill: /etc/claude-code/.claude/skills/data-analysis

# Data Analysis

## Python Environment Setup

Common data analysis packages are **cached in the image** for instant installation. Initialize a Python project in the workspace if one doesn't exist:

```bash
uv init --python 3.13
uv add pandas numpy polars matplotlib altair plotly seaborn scipy scikit-learn duckdb pyarrow jupyterlab
```

Run scripts and tools with `uv run`:
```bash
uv run python script.py
uv run jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute --inplace notebook.ipynb
```

Add more packages with `uv add <package>`. The project's `pyproject.toml` and `.venv` persist across sessions. Skip `uv init` if `pyproject.toml` already exists.

`google-cloud-bigquery`, `google-cloud-bigquery-storage`, and `google-auth` are cached in the image for fast `uv add`, but they are not preinstalled in the shared base interpreter.

## Database CLI

### usql - Universal SQL CLI

```bash
# PostgreSQL
usql postgres://user:pass@host:5432/dbname

# MySQL
usql mysql://user:pass@host:3306/dbname

# SQLite
usql sqlite:./data.db

# SQL Server
usql sqlserver://user:pass@host/instance/dbname

# BigQuery — use Python client with access token instead (see BigQuery section below)
# usql bigquery:// is NOT recommended; use the google-cloud-bigquery Python client
```

Common commands inside usql:
- `\dt` - List tables
- `\d tablename` - Describe table
- `\q` - Quit

### sqlite3

```bash
sqlite3 data.db "SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10"
```

## Python Data Processing

### Core Libraries

| Package | Purpose | Status |
|---------|---------|--------|
| `pandas` | DataFrames and data manipulation | cached |
| `numpy` | Numerical computing | cached |
| `polars` | Fast DataFrame library (Rust-based) | cached |
| `duckdb` | In-process SQL analytics | cached |

```python
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
import duckdb

# Pandas
df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
df.groupby("category").sum()

# Polars (faster for large data)
df = pl.read_csv("data.csv")
df.group_by("category").agg(pl.sum("amount"))

# DuckDB - SQL on files directly
result = duckdb.sql("SELECT * FROM 'data.csv' WHERE amount > 1000")
print(result.df())
```

### Visualization

camelAI's notebook preview renders Altair and Plotly charts natively — not in iframes. Chart colors, text, and backgrounds automatically adapt to the user's light/dark theme.

**Preferred order:**
1. **Altair** (Vega-Lite) — emits structured specs with full theme support
2. **Plotly** — also renders natively; use when Altair doesn't cover the chart type (3D, maps, financial)
3. **matplotlib / seaborn** — static PNG fallback; won't adapt to dark mode

| Package | Purpose | Status |
|---------|---------|--------|
| `altair` | Declarative charts (Vega-Lite) — **preferred** | cached |
| `plotly` | Interactive charts — use for 3D, maps, finance | cached |
| `matplotlib` | Static plots (fallback) | cached |
| `seaborn` | Statistical visualization (fallback) | cached |

```python
# Altair (preferred — renders natively with dark/light theme support)
import altair as alt

chart = alt.Chart(df).mark_bar().encode(
    x="category:N",
    y="amount:Q"
).properties(
    title=alt.Title("Sales by Category", subtitle="Q4 2025 data"),
    width=500,
    height=300
)
chart  # Display in notebook cell output

# Plotly (native rendering, use for charts Altair doesn't support)
import plotly.express as px
fig = px.line(df, x="date", y="value", title="Trend Over Time")
fig.show()

# Matplotlib/Seaborn (static PNG — no dark mode support)
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
sns.barplot(data=df, x="category", y="amount")
plt.savefig("barplot.png")
```

**Altair renderer constraints:**
- Use `alt.Title("Title", subtitle="Subtitle")` — both are themed automatically
- Do **not** set `background` — the renderer makes backgrounds transparent
- Do **not** hardcode text colors — the renderer applies theme-appropriate colors
- Set `width`/`height` via `.properties()` — width is overridden to fill the container; height is used as a baseline
- Arc marks (donut/pie) are detected and allocated extra vertical space automatically

**Plotly renderer constraints:**
- Use `fig.show()` to emit Plotly MIME output — the renderer picks it up natively
- Do **not** use `fig.write_image()` or `fig.write_html()` — these bypass native rendering
- Do **not** set `paper_bgcolor` or `plot_bgcolor` — the renderer makes them transparent
- Subtitles via `layout.annotations` are automatically themed

### Tabular output

When outputting tabular data in notebooks, use plain pandas DataFrames — not `df.style` (pandas Styler). The rendering environment handles table styling automatically with theme-aware colors, index columns, and overflow handling.

**Never output tables as raw HTML** (e.g., manually constructing `<table>` tags or using `IPython.display.HTML("<table>...")`). Always use pandas DataFrames for tabular output — the rendering environment detects DataFrames automatically and applies theme-aware styling, sortable columns, row filtering, and CSV export. Raw HTML tables bypass all of this and render unstyled in an iframe.

The sandbox pre-configures pandas to show up to 200 rows and 200 characters per cell in notebook HTML output. You do not need to add `pd.set_option` calls for display limits unless the user asks for different values.

Only use `df.style` when the user explicitly requests conditional formatting, cell-level color coding, or other per-cell visual logic that can't be achieved with a plain table.

## Jupyter Notebook Workflow (Preferred)

For exploratory analysis, prefer delivering results as a Jupyter notebook (`.ipynb`) instead of a standalone `.py` script with separate chart/image files. Notebooks combine code, visual output, and markdown conclusions in one artifact.

### Build notebooks incrementally

- Keep a narrative flow:
  - markdown cell: objective and dataset context
  - code cell: data loading/cleaning
  - markdown cell: what to look for
  - code cell: chart/query
  - markdown cell: interpretation and takeaway

### Execute notebooks

```bash
uv run jupyter nbconvert --to notebook --execute --inplace analysis.ipynb
```

**Always validate after execution.** Run the notebook validator to catch errors that `nbconvert` may not surface (cell exceptions, charts that fell back to text/plain, blank charts with constant data):

```bash
validate-notebook analysis.ipynb
```

If it reports issues, fix the failing cells and re-execute. Do **not** use `--allow-errors` — it silently embeds tracebacks in cell outputs that the user will see in the rendered report.

### Preview notebooks in chat

After creating or updating a notebook, set the active chat preview to the notebook file:

```text
set_file_preview(
  path="/home/claude/analysis.ipynb",
  content_type="application/x-ipynb+json"
)
```

### Publish files as standalone apps

When a user wants to publish a notebook (or any file) as a standalone app, deploy with:

```bash
publish my-notebook-app --file /path/to/analysis.ipynb
```

This deploys a lightweight Cloudflare Worker that serves the file via the main app's embed viewer. No build step required.

### How notebooks are presented

camelAI renders notebooks in **Report mode** by default — the user sees a polished article, not raw cells.

**What Report mode does:**
- Hides all code — only markdown prose and cell outputs (charts, tables, text) are visible
- Auto-hides setup cells (imports, data loading, `.describe()`, `pd.set_option`, etc.)
- Extracts the first `#` heading as the report title and the following paragraph as the subtitle
- Builds a sidebar table of contents from `##` and `###` headings

**Structure notebooks for Report mode:**
- Start with a single `#` heading followed by a one-sentence description (becomes the report header)
- Use `##` headings to define sections — these populate the sidebar TOC
- Keep setup code in dedicated cells (the classifier hides entire cells, not individual lines)
- Write markdown between analysis cells explaining what each result shows
- End with a `## Key Findings` or `## Conclusion` section

The user can toggle to Notebook mode to see all cells, code, and execution counts, but Report mode is the default first impression.

### SQL Server / PostgreSQL / MySQL (Primary: Worker `DATA_PROXY` service binding)

For deployed/user-uploaded Cloudflare Workers, use the `DATA_PROXY` service binding first.
This is the most important path because Workers may not be able to use native DB drivers/TCP connectivity directly.

Read example:

```typescript
const readResult = await context.cloudflare.env.DATA_PROXY.postgresQuery({
  mode: "read",
  host: "db.example.com",
  user: "user",
  password: "pass",
  database: "analytics",
  query: "SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE id =  LIMIT 100",
  params: [123],
});

if (!readResult.ok) throw new Error(readResult.error.message);
const rows = readResult.data.recordset ?? [];
```

Modify example:

```typescript
const modifyResult = await context.cloudflare.env.DATA_PROXY.postgresQuery({
  mode: "modify",
  host: "db.example.com",
  user: "user",
  password: "pass",
  database: "analytics",
  query: "UPDATE users SET last_seen_at = NOW() WHERE id = ",
  params: [123],
});

if (!modifyResult.ok) throw new Error(modifyResult.error.message);
const affected = modifyResult.data.rowsAffected?.[0] ?? 0;
```

Supported query methods:
- `DATA_PROXY.mssqlQuery(...)` (named params, e.g. `@id`)
- `DATA_PROXY.postgresQuery(...)` (positional params array)
- `DATA_PROXY.mysqlQuery(...)` (positional params array)
- All query calls require `mode: "read"` or `mode: "modify"` (no auto-detection).

### Sandbox/container scripts (secondary: `DATA_PROXY_URL`)

Inside sandbox/container scripts, you can call the HTTP proxy via `DATA_PROXY_URL`.
No bearer token is required; requests are authenticated by sandbox-host identity headers.
Keep queries bounded (`LIMIT`, selective `WHERE`) to avoid very large responses.

```python
import os
import pandas as pd
import requests

data_proxy_url = os.environ["DATA_PROXY_URL"].rstrip("/")

postgres = requests.post(
    f"{data_proxy_url}/postgres/query",
    json={
        "mode": "read",
        "host": "your-postgres-host",
        "user": "username",
        "password": "password",
        "database": "analytics",
        "query": "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id =  ORDER BY id LIMIT 100",
        "params": [123],
        "sslmode": "require",
    },
    timeout=60,
).json()

df = pd.DataFrame(postgres.get("recordset", []))
print(df.head())

postgres_modify = requests.post(
    f"{data_proxy_url}/postgres/query",
    json={
        "mode": "modify",
        "host": "your-postgres-host",
        "user": "username",
        "password": "password",
        "database": "analytics",
        "query": "UPDATE users SET last_seen_at = NOW() WHERE id = ",
        "params": [123],
        "sslmode": "require",
    },
    timeout=60,
).json()

rows_affected = (postgres_modify.get("rowsAffected") or [0])[0]
print(rows_affected)
```

### Direct drivers (preferred local fallback in containers)

For sandbox/container code, native drivers are the primary fallback when proxy access is unnecessary.
Use this path when the user explicitly asks for direct connectivity or when local direct access is simpler.

```python
from sqlalchemy import create_engine
import pandas as pd

# PostgreSQL direct
pg_engine = create_engine("postgresql+psycopg://user:pass@host/db")
pg_df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM users", pg_engine)

# MySQL direct
mysql_engine = create_engine("mysql+pymysql://user:pass@host/db")
mysql_df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM orders", mysql_engine)
```

### BigQuery

**Important:** BigQuery connections in camelAI use **OAuth access tokens**, not service account JSON files directly. The platform automatically generates short-lived access tokens from the user's service account JSON key and exposes them as environment variables. Always use this token-based approach.

When a BigQuery integration named e.g. "Production" is connected, these env vars are available:
- `INT_BIGQUERY_PRODUCTION_ACCESS_TOKEN` — short-lived OAuth token (auto-refreshed by the platform)
- `INT_BIGQUERY_PRODUCTION_PROJECT_ID` — the GCP project ID

```python
import os
from google.cloud import bigquery
from google.oauth2.credentials import Credentials

# Get the access token and project from environment variables.
# Replace PRODUCTION with the actual integration name (uppercased, non-alphanumeric → underscores).
access_token = os.environ["INT_BIGQUERY_PRODUCTION_ACCESS_TOKEN"]
project_id = os.environ["INT_BIGQUERY_PRODUCTION_PROJECT_ID"]

# Create client using the OAuth access token — do NOT use from_service_account_json()
credentials = Credentials(token=access_token)
client = bigquery.Client(project=project_id, credentials=credentials)

df = client.query("SELECT * FROM dataset.table").to_dataframe()
```

**Do NOT** use `bigquery.Client.from_service_account_json()` or try to read a service account JSON file. The raw service account key is never available in the container — only the derived access token is exposed.
